focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The next afternoon, every half hour Gavin had to stop reading and press his fingers against his thighs until the joints loosened.

The sub-collection was colder in December. The chill came out of the limestone itself, dry and mineral, nothing to do with the weather outside, and it made his left hand tremor harder — the same fine shake the mass detention had left behind, sharpened now until the manuscript's notation blurred when he held the pages up to the wall's glow.

He sat on the floor with his back against the shelves, the manuscript spread across his knees, his notebook open beside him on the stone. The passage had been defeating him for three days.

The notation was doing something he hadn't seen before. The earlier sections — the ones describing the First Art's principles, magic as agreements that could be renegotiated, the substrate beneath spells — had used symbols that moved in pairs and small groups, showing direct correspondences between concepts. This section's symbols moved in larger patterns. Slow, layered, interconnected. When he translated one cluster and wrote the meaning down, the act of comprehension seemed to shift the patterns around it, as though the page were adjusting to his understanding. He wasn't sure if the adjustment was in the manuscript or in his perception. The distinction had been getting less clear for weeks.

He'd been translating the section using Babbling's framework — structural invariants, repeating patterns, mapping known elements onto unknown positions — and it worked, but slowly. Each evening he got a few more sentences into English, or something near English. The translations sat in his notebook in his own bad handwriting, crossed out and rewritten and crossed out again.

The section was about origins. Not the Fir...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...essure snapped tight around the spoon, and Gavin flinched before he knew he was moving. His knuckles clipped the egg instead. It spun off the plate, hit the bench, and burst on the flagstones by his shoe. Euan stopped talking long enough to glance over. Miren's hand stayed in the air between them for a second longer than it should have.

Gavin stared at the split shell and the yellow spreading into the grooves of the stone. The smell hit him at once, sharp and sulphurous. Somewhere farther down the table the first-year whispered the charm again, and the pressure around the spoon went tight enough to pinch his vision at the edges. "I'm fine," he said, still looking at the floor.

No one answered. Miren drew her hand back slowly. Gavin stood because if he stayed for one more lift of the spoon or one more flare from the candles he was going to be sick on the Hufflepuff bench. He left the egg where it was and the toast untouched, and he could feel her still watching him as he walked toward the doors. The Hall's pressure followed him into the corridor and only thinned when the doors shut behind him.

He stopped just outside with one hand on the stone wall and waited for the corridor to become a corridor again instead of a stack of overlapping strains. His mouth watered with the warning before vomiting, but nothing came up. A pair of Ravenclaws hurried past toward Charms, and the little enchantments on their ink-stained bags left wakes in the air he could almost track. By the time he made himself turn toward the greenhouses, his hands had stopped shaking enough that no one glancing at him would notice. He knew he wasn't taking the manuscript back into the Great Hall. If he opened it again, it would be behind his bed curtains, where the castle's enchantments pressed less hard.
